claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 7 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 20 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 7 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 20, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have three children that I'd like to check eligibility for. Their names are Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you check eligibility for Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

Let me start by asking: **How old are Alex, Blake, and Charlie?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is 7, Blake is 20, and Charlie is also 7 - Alex and Charlie are twins.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

Now I need to ask: **Do Alex, Blake, and Charlie all live with you in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they all live with me here in the UK. I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now regarding Blake, who is 20 years old - **Is Blake currently in approved education or training?**

(This is important because Child Benefit normally only covers children under 16, or under 20 if they're in approved education or training.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't in education or training. He actually works full-time at a local warehouse.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Just to confirm about Blake's work situation: **How many hours per week does Blake work at the warehouse?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He works full-time - about 35 hours a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now I need to ask about your income situation: **Do you or your partner (if you have one) have an individual income over £60,000 per year?**

(This won't affect whether you're eligible for Child Benefit, but it may mean you have to pay a tax charge on it.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I don't have a partner, and no, my income is well below £60,000.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Let me just confirm one final detail: **Are you responsible for all three children - meaning you're providing for their needs such as food, clothes, and accommodation?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm responsible for all three of them. I provide for their food, clothes, and everything they need at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Summary of Your Child Benefit Eligibility

Based on the information you've provided, here's the outcome for each of your children:

✅ **Alex (age 7) - ELIGIBLE**
You qualify for Child Benefit for Alex because they are under 16, live with you in the UK, and you're responsible for their care.

❌ **Blake (age 20) - NOT ELIGIBLE**
Unfortunately, you do not qualify for Child Benefit for Blake. This is because Blake is 20 years old and not in approved education or training. Child Benefit can only continue for young people aged 16-19 if they're in approved education or training. Additionally, Blake works 35 hours per week (more than the 24-hour threshold), which would stop Child Benefit eligibility even if they were younger.

✅ **Charlie (age 7) - ELIGIBLE**
You qualify for Child Benefit for Charlie because they are under 16, live with you in the UK, and you're responsible for their care.

**In summary:** You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex and Charlie, but not for Blake. Since your income is below £60,000, you won't have to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge.

You can apply for Child Benefit for Alex and Charlie if you haven't already done so.

reasonings: Alex is 7 years old and lives with the claimant in the UK. The claimant is responsible for Alex, providing for their food, clothes, and accommodation. Under the Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. Alex meets all these criteria, so the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 20 years old and works full-time (35 hours per week) at a warehouse. Blake is not in approved education or training. Under the Child Benefit rules, you can claim for a child aged 16-19 only if they stay in approved education or training. Since Blake is 20 years old and not in approved education or training, and is working 24 hours or more per week, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake. The rules specifically state that Child Benefit stops if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training.

Charlie is 7 years old and lives with the claimant in the UK. The claimant is responsible for Charlie, providing for their food, clothes, and accommodation. Under the Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. Charlie meets all these criteria, so the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has three children: Alex (7), Blake (20), and Charlie (7). The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) and is responsible for all three children. Alex and Charlie are both 7 years old and under 16, so they qualify for Child Benefit. Blake is 20 years old and works full-time (35 hours per week), and is not in approved education or training. Since Blake exceeds the age limit for Child Benefit (under 20 only if in approved education/training) and works 24+ hours per week, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake. The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for 2 out of 3 children (Alex and Charlie).
